en.extract:
The Okarito kiwi (Apteryx rowi), also known as the rowi or Okarito brown kiwi, is a member of the kiwi family Apterygidae, described as new to science in 2003. The species is part of the brown kiwi complex, and is morphologically very similar to other members of that complex. It is found in a restricted area of the Ōkārito forest on the West Coast of New Zealand's South Island, and has a population of only about 600 birds.


Taxonomy
The Okarito kiwi is a monotypic species, i.e. there are no recognised subspecies. The genus name Apteryx stems from the Greek "without wings". Originally assumed to be the same species as the Southern brown kiwi A. australis, DNA testing shows that the possible split off from this species was 8.2 million years ago, and the split from their closest relatives, the Northern Island brown kiwi A. mantelli was around 6.2 million years ago. This bird is a ratite and has similarities to the others (emu, ostrich, rhea, cassowary). Its sternum has no keel, its wings are minimal, and it has no preen gland. Its palate is also distinctive, and its feathers have no barbules or aftershaft.  Other features that are similar to only the other kiwi is a weak gizzard and no tail, only a pygostyle.
The taxonomic status of the Okarito Forest population of kiwi has been debated since the 1950s, first by Brian Reid and Colin Roderick of the New Zealand Wildlife Service, who claimed the population was distinct from the kiwi found in Fiordland. The presence of a distinct kiwi led to the southern Okarito Forest becoming a protected area in the 1970s, and later being integrated into Westland Tai Poutini National Park. Recognition of the Okarito kiwi being a separate taxon slowly gained recognition since the early 1990s. Indications that the population was a distinct species included DNA evidence undertaken by Allan J. Baker in 1995 showing great variation in the genetics of kiwi populations, and the discovery of Apterygon okarito, a louse that exclusively uses the Okarito kiwi as a host. The species was first formally described in 2003 by ornithologists Alan Tennyson, Ricardo L. Palma, Hugh A. Robertson, Trevor Worthy and Brian J. Gill.


Range and habitat
The Okarito brown kiwi lives in the Okarito forest on the West Coast of New Zealand's South Island.
On 29 June 2010, three breeding pairs were released onto Blumine Island as part of a breeding programme. In 2012, twenty rowi were released on Mana Island. A new population was established in the Omoeroa Ranges near Fox Glacier in late 2018. In 2021 signs of rowi were detected in Ballyhooly Bush, remnant mataī forest on the Lower Whataroa River flats, 24 km from Ōkārito forest.


Reproduction
The female can lay up to three eggs, each in a different nest. Both the male and the female incubate the eggs. The egg is very large, as it weighs 20% of the female's weight (as in all kiwi). The eggs incubate for 75–85 days. Most pairs are monogamous throughout their lives.


Status and conservation
The Okarito kiwi is currently classified as Vulnerable by the IUCN due to habitat loss and predation by introduced stoats. Conservation efforts such as Operation Nest Egg and the stoat control regime have been partially successful in restoring the rowi population. However, the rowi is still in a fragile stage of existence. Predation, mainly from imported animals such as stoats, is still the biggest threat to the rowi. The South Okarito Forest was designated a kiwi sanctuary in 2000.
The West Coast Wildlife Centre in Franz Josef village operates a conservation programme as part of Operation Nest Egg.  Eggs at risk of predation are removed, the chicks hatched in captivity, raised in a natural predator-free environment until old enough to fend for themselves, and then returned to the wild. The operation opened in 2010 and has been responsible for raising the wild population of rowi from just 165 ageing adults in the 1990s to 600 as of 2019. Surveys have ensured that there is no noticeable difference in behaviour between such birds and rowi growing up fully in the wild. The Wildlife Centre is the only place in New Zealand where one can see rowi in a nocturnal walkthrough area.


Gallery


References


External links

West Coast Wildlife Centre

The '''Okarito kiwi''' ('''''Apteryx rowi'''''), also known as the '''rowi''' or '''Okarito brown kiwi''', is a member of the [[Kiwi (bird)|kiwi]] family [[Apterygidae]], described as [[Bird species new to science|new to science]] in 2003.<ref name=tennyson/> The species is part of the [[brown kiwi]] complex, and is [[morphology (biology)|morphologically]] very similar to other members of that complex. It is found in a restricted area of the [[Ōkārito]] forest on the [[West Coast, New Zealand|West Coast]] of [[New Zealand]]'s [[South Island]],<ref name="Clements"/> and has a population of only about 600 birds.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.odt.co.nz/regions/west-coast/rare-rowi-kiwi-be-released-westland|title=Rare rowi kiwi to be released in Westland|last=Mills|first=Laura|date=2018-12-13|website=[[Otago Daily Times]] |language=en|access-date=2019-10-26}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
The Okarito kiwi is a monotypic species, i.e. there are no recognised subspecies.<ref name="Clements" /> The genus name ''[[Apteryx]]'' stems from the Greek "without wings".<ref name=gotch/> Originally assumed to be the same species as the [[Southern brown kiwi]] ''A. australis'', DNA testing shows that the possible split off from this species was 8.2 million years ago, and the split from their closest relatives, the Northern Island brown kiwi ''[[A. mantelli]]'' was around 6.2 million years ago.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Brewer |first1=David |title=Birds New to Science: Fifty Years of Avian Discovery |date=2018 |publisher=Christopher Helm |location=London |isbn=978-1-4729-0628-1 |page=22 |language=en}}</ref> This bird is a [[ratite]] and has similarities to the others ([[emu]], [[ostrich]], [[Rhea (bird)|rhea]], [[cassowary]]). Its [[sternum]] has no keel, its wings are minimal, and it has no [[preen gland]]. Its [[palate]] is also distinctive, and its feathers have no [[barbule]]s or aftershaft.  Other features that are similar to only the other [[Kiwi (bird)|kiwi]] is a weak [[gizzard]] and no tail, only a [[pygostyle]].<ref name="Davies"/>

The taxonomic status of the Okarito Forest population of kiwi has been debated since the 1950s, first by Brian Reid and Colin Roderick of the New Zealand Wildlife Service, who claimed the population was distinct from the kiwi found in [[Fiordland]]. The presence of a distinct kiwi led to the southern Okarito Forest becoming a protected area in the 1970s, and later being integrated into [[Westland Tai Poutini National Park]].<ref name=tennyson/> Recognition of the Okarito kiwi being a separate taxon slowly gained recognition since the early 1990s. Indications that the population was a distinct species included DNA evidence undertaken by [[Allan J. Baker]] in 1995 showing great variation in the genetics of kiwi populations,<ref name=tennyson/><ref>{{cite Q|Q28776040}}</ref> and the discovery of ''[[Apterygon okarito]]'', a louse that exclusively uses the Okarito kiwi as a host.<ref name=tennyson/><ref>{{cite Q|Q54594669}}</ref> The species was first formally described in 2003 by ornithologists Alan Tennyson, Ricardo L. Palma, Hugh A. Robertson, [[Trevor Worthy]] and Brian J. Gill.<ref name=tennyson/>

==Range and habitat==
The Okarito brown kiwi lives in the Okarito forest on the West Coast of New Zealand's South Island.<ref name="Clements" /><ref name="DOC"/>

On 29 June 2010, three breeding pairs were released onto [[Blumine Island]] as part of a breeding programme.<ref name="Stuff.co.nz_3869779">{{cite news |url=http://www.stuff.co.nz/national/3869779/Kiwi-released-on-Blumine-Island |title=Kiwi released on Blumine Island |date=30 June 2010 |work=[[The Marlborough Express]] |access-date=1 November 2011}}</ref> In 2012, twenty rowi were released on [[Mana Island (New Zealand)|Mana Island]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=History of Mana Island {{!}} Friends of Mana Island |url=https://manaisland.org.nz/mana-island-2/history/ |access-date=2025-03-20 |language=en-NZ}}</ref> A new population was established in the Omoeroa Ranges near [[Fox Glacier / Te Moeka o Tuawe|Fox Glacier]] in late 2018.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Naish|first=Joanne|date=2 September 2021|title=New chick a sign of hope for rarest kiwi|page=9|work=[[The Press]]}}</ref> In 2021 signs of rowi were detected in Ballyhooly Bush, remnant [[Prumnopitys taxifolia|mataī]] forest on the Lower Whataroa River flats, 24 km from Ōkārito forest.<ref>{{Cite news |date=25 August 2021 |title=Rare kiwi found at Whataroa |page=1 |work=[[Greymouth Star]] }}</ref>

==Reproduction==
The female can lay up to three [[Egg (biology)|eggs]], each in a different [[bird nest|nest]]. Both the male and the female incubate the eggs. The egg is very large, as it weighs 20% of the female's weight (as in all kiwi). The eggs incubate for 75–85 days.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Sales |first1=James |date=2005 |title=The endangered kiwi: A review |journal=Folia Zoologica |volume=54 |number=1–2 |pages=1–20 |url=https://phthiraptera.myspecies.info/content/endangered-kiwi-review}}</ref> Most pairs are monogamous throughout their lives.<ref name="DOC"/>

==Status and conservation==
The Okarito kiwi is currently classified as [[Vulnerable species|Vulnerable]] by the [[IUCN]] due to habitat loss and predation by introduced stoats.<ref name="iucn status 18 November 2021" /> Conservation efforts such as Operation Nest Egg and the stoat control regime have been partially successful in restoring the rowi population. However, the rowi is still in a fragile stage of existence. [[Predation]], mainly from imported animals such as [[stoats]], is still the biggest threat to the rowi. The South Okarito Forest was designated a kiwi sanctuary in 2000.<ref name="DOC"/>

The [[West Coast Wildlife Centre]] in Franz Josef village operates a conservation programme as part of Operation Nest Egg.  Eggs at risk of predation are removed, the chicks hatched in captivity, raised in a natural predator-free environment until old enough to fend for themselves, and then returned to the wild. The operation opened in 2010 and has been responsible for raising the wild population of rowi from just 165 ageing adults in the 1990s to 600 {{as of|2019|lc=y}}.<ref name=":1">{{Cite news|last=Waterworth|first=Kerrie|date=9 February 2018|title=Rescuing rowi|page=8|work=[[Greymouth Star]]}}</ref> Surveys have ensured that there is no noticeable difference in behaviour between such birds and rowi growing up fully in the wild. The Wildlife Centre is the only place in New Zealand where one can see rowi in a nocturnal walkthrough area.<ref>{{cite news|publisher=[[The Press]]|date=20 July 2012|first1=Anna |last1=Turner|url=http://www.stuff.co.nz/the-press/news/7318181/Influx-of-kiwi-eggs-forces-centre-expansion|title=Influx of kiwi eggs forces centre expansion|access-date=19 March 2015}}</ref>

zh.extract:
白眶几维（台湾作奧卡里多褐鷸鴕）（学名：Apteryx rowi），是鹬鸵目鹬鸵科的一种鸟类，栖息在密集的高大的树木组成的森林中。白眶几维体重1924克，翼长0.1毫米，嘴峰长157毫米，喙宽度4.7毫米，喙厚度5.3毫米，跗蹠长84毫米，尾长0.1毫米。白眶几维在其分布区内为留鸟，其食性为肉食性，主要食物来源是陆生无脊椎动物。分布于奥卡里托森林（新西兰南岛西海岸）。
